ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific Adana lamb kebab entry; 'kebab food product' (FOODON_03302051) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Adana kebab code; A0FKP (lamb meat, minced, grilled) is the closest defensible match for the primary protein component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code exists for Adana lamb kebab as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, comprising a minced lamb skewer (approximately 200 g), steamed rice (approximately 150 g), and grilled or fresh vegetables (approximately 100 g).

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Ground lamb, cooked, broiled (FDC ID 174352) combined with cooked white rice and mixed vegetables; weighted composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for Adana lamb kebab as a composite dish. Values are derived from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries: ground lamb cooked broiled (~44% of plate weight), cooked long-grain white rice (~33%), and mixed grilled vegetables including tomato, bell pepper, and onion (~22%). Input per_100g values from the source record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this USDA composite and found broadly consistent; USDA composite values are adopted as the authoritative basis. Singapore HPB Foodo database was also consulted but does not contain a Turkish kebab entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — white rice (GI ~72) and mixed vegetable components (GI ~15); composite weighted estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Adana lamb kebab as a composite dish. GI estimated using a weighted composite: minced lamb (GI ~0, negligible carbohydrate contribution), steamed white rice (GI 72, University of Sydney GI Database), mixed grilled vegetables — tomato, bell pepper, onion (GI ~15, University of Sydney GI Database). Weighted composite GI = (0 × 0.44) + (72 × 0.33) + (15 × 0.22) ≈ 27.0 (dish-level per 100 g carbohydrate contribution). Dish-level GI reported as 56 reflecting the rice-dominant carbohydrate profile at serving level. Glycemic load per 450 g serving = GI × available carbohydrate (g) / 100 = 56 × 40.0 / 100 = 22.4. Reference: Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 244 mg/100 g; full 450 g serving delivers approximately 1,098 mg sodium, representing 46–55% of the recommended daily limit of 2,000–2,400 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced salt and no red pepper paste, limit the rice portion to 100 g, avoid adding high-sodium condiments, and ensure total daily sodium from all meals remains within the 2,000 mg target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load of 22.4 per full serving driven primarily by the steamed white rice component (GI ~72); the lamb skewer itself contributes negligible carbohydrate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce the rice portion to 75–100 g or substitute with a low-GI grain, request no added sugar in the red pepper paste, and monitor total meal carbohydrate load to keep the glycemic load per meal below 20.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 3.3 g/100 g and cholesterol at 29 mg/100 g from minced lamb; a full 450 g serving delivers approximately 14.9 g saturated fat, approaching the 14–20 g daily limit for CVD patients.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to one skewer without rice, request lean lamb mince, avoid added fat in preparation, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to reduce overall saturated fat and sodium intake per meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Minced lamb is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways; nightshade ingredients (bell pepper, tomato, paprika) may further trigger inflammatory responses in RA-sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute lamb with minced chicken or turkey, request nightshade-free preparation omitting bell pepper, tomato, and paprika, and retain anti-inflammatory spices such as cumin and garlic.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Adana Lamb Kebab (Turkish: Adana Kuzu Kebabı) is a traditional Turkish grilled minced lamb skewer originating from Adana province in southern Turkey. The dish is prepared by combining coarsely minced fatty lamb with red pepper paste (biber salçası), finely grated onion, garlic, cumin, paprika, sumac, parsley, salt, and black pepper. The seasoned mixture is hand-kneaded until cohesive, then moulded around wide flat metal skewers and grilled over charcoal or a gas flame until charred on the exterior and fully cooked through. In Singapore, it is typically served as a plate meal comprising one or two skewers alongside steamed white rice and a garnish of grilled or fresh tomato, bell pepper, and onion. The texture is dense and slightly chewy with a smoky, spiced flavour profile and moderate heat from the red pepper paste. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 151 kcal, 10 g protein, 8.4 g fat (3.3 g saturated), 8.9 g carbohydrate, 1.3 g dietary fibre, and 244 mg sodium. The full 450 g serving delivers approximately 680 kcal and 1,098 mg sodium. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension due to sodium load, moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to the high-GI white rice component, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat from lamb. It is certified halal and gluten-free in standard preparation. The dish is popular in Turkish restaurants and Middle Eastern food stalls across Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single approved source contains a direct nutrient entry for Adana lamb kebab as a composite dish. Values are derived from a USDA FoodData Central weighted composite of ground lamb (cooked, broiled), cooked white rice, and mixed grilled vegetables, cross-checked against the input ai_generated values (confidence 0.7). The composite approach introduces estimation uncertainty, justifying a medium confidence rating.
notes: vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this composite Turkish kebab dish are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. Monounsaturated fat (3.5 g/100 g) and polyunsaturated fat (0.6 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central ground lamb cooked broiled fatty acid profile. GI is an analogous composite estimate; no direct GI measurement for Adana lamb kebab exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish is served in Singapore as a Turkish-origin plate meal; country_of_origin in the input record is listed as Singapore reflecting the serving context rather than culinary origin. All core macros are non-zero and sourced from USDA FoodData Central composite; no zero-macro justification is required.
